India would support a similar normative framework negotiated in the General Assembly or through the OEWG with universal application and without the veto shield. We abstain on this text while supporting its underlying principles.
keyPoints: Victim of state cyber operations but concerns on mechanism
Attribution technically complex and inherently uncertain
SC veto creates asymmetry shielding some from findings

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, France considers the protection of civilian critical infrastructure from state-sponsored cyber operations to be one of the defining security challenges of our era. The attack on the French hospital system in 2024, attributed to state-linked actors, demonstrated that cyber operations can directly endanger human life. This is not hypothetical; it is present reality.

France strongly supports this resolution. We particularly welcome the affirmation in the preamble that international humanitarian law applies to cyber operations. This is not new law; it is the application of existing legal principles to a new domain. States that conduct cyber operations against hospitals, water systems, and power grids during armed conflict are violating the laws of war, and must be held accountable.

Regarding the attribution panel, France notes that national attribution has already become standard practice among responsible states. France has publicly attributed cyber operations when evidence supported such assessment. An international panel operating with technical rigor and political independence would enhance credibility and reduce the politicization of attribution. France supports the resolution and calls upon all Council members to demonstrate that the Security Council can adapt to twenty-first century threats.
keyPoints: Hospital cyber attack demonstrated direct threat to life
IHL applies to cyber operations - existing law, new domain
International attribution enhances credibility over national

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Brazil has been one of the most active voices in the Open-Ended Working Group on ICTs in the context of international security. We have consistently advocated for a rules-based approach to cyberspace that protects all states equally, particularly those with less developed cyber defence capabilities.

Brazil supports this resolution with one important caveat. The revelations of 2013 demonstrated that mass surveillance of foreign governments and populations by allied intelligence agencies is as much a violation of sovereignty as a destructive cyber attack. Any framework for responsible state behaviour in cyberspace must ultimately address the full spectrum of harmful activities, not only those that damage infrastructure.

That said, the protection of civilian critical infrastructure is an appropriate starting point. Brazil, which has experienced cyber operations targeting our electoral infrastructure and state petroleum company, understands the urgency. We support the attribution panel and the graduated sanctions framework as mechanisms that, if designed with genuine political independence and technical competence, could deter the most egregious violations. Brazil votes Yes and calls for the panel's mandate to be interpreted broadly to encompass espionage against critical infrastructure, not only destructive operations.
keyPoints: Mass surveillance equally violates sovereignty
Experienced attacks on electoral and petroleum infrastructure
Panel mandate should cover espionage, not only destruction

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Estonia speaks from direct experience. In 2007, our nation endured a coordinated cyber campaign that targeted government, banking, media, and telecommunications simultaneously. Our digital infrastructure, upon which our e-government model depends, was subjected to an assault designed to paralyze our state. Estonia learned in 2007 what this resolution seeks to prevent from becoming routine.

Since then, Estonia has been at the forefront of international cyber governance. We host the NATO Cooperative Cyber Defence Centre of Excellence and have contributed extensively to the Tallinn Manual process clarifying international law's application to cyberspace. We can state with authority that the norms in this resolution are both legally sound and operationally necessary.

Estonia strongly supports the attribution panel. Attribution is not impossible; it is politically inconvenient for aggressors. Technical forensics, combined with signals intelligence and contextual analysis, have proven repeatedly capable of identifying state-sponsored actors with high confidence. What has been lacking is not capability but the political will to act on attributions. This resolution provides the institutional framework for that will. Estonia votes Yes and calls upon all states that have experienced state-sponsored cyber operations to join in demanding accountability.
keyPoints: 2007 cyber assault paralyzed government and banking
Attribution not impossible, just politically inconvenient
Technical forensics can identify actors with high confidence

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, the Russian Federation categorically rejects this resolution as a transparent attempt to establish a Western-controlled cyber enforcement mechanism under Security Council authority. Russia notes with irony that the states most vocal about cyber norms are those whose intelligence agencies have been documented conducting the most extensive signals intelligence operations in human history.

Russia has consistently advocated for an international information security treaty negotiated through the United Nations with universal participation. We have tabled draft conventions dating back to 1998. These proposals were rejected by the very states now sponsoring this resolution, because a universal treaty would constrain their own activities equally. The selective enforcement mechanism proposed here is designed to apply pressure asymmetrically.

The attribution panel concept is fundamentally flawed. Cyber attribution is not a technical problem; it is a political problem. False flag operations are routine. Evidence can be planted. And a panel composed of experts from states with advanced offensive capabilities will inevitably reflect their biases. Russia proposes instead a universal ICT security treaty with equal obligations, negotiated in the OEWG, without selective enforcement through the Security Council. We exercise our right of veto if this resolution proceeds to a vote.
keyPoints: Western-controlled enforcement applied asymmetrically
Has proposed universal treaties since 1998, rejected by sponsors
Threatens veto, calls attribution fundamentally political

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Brazil returns to address the veto threat issued by the Russian Federation. It is precisely this type of scenario that demonstrates the paralysis of the Security Council on emerging threats. A state accused of conducting aggressive cyber operations exercises its veto to prevent the establishment of accountability mechanisms. The circularity is self-evident.

Brazil reaffirms its support for this resolution while acknowledging that the veto threat may render it operationally ineffective. We call upon the sponsors to prepare a parallel track: should the Council be paralyzed, the General Assembly can and should establish a similar normative framework under its own authority, including an attribution mechanism operating with moral rather than enforcement authority.

The Uniting for Peace precedent demonstrates that Council paralysis need not equate to UN paralysis. Brazil also calls upon states that abstained in the first round due to concerns about Council dynamics to reconsider. A strong affirmative vote, even if vetoed, sends an unambiguous normative signal. The international community must demonstrate that state-sponsored attacks on hospitals and water systems are unacceptable, regardless of whether a single veto prevents formal enforcement.
keyPoints: Veto circularity: accused state blocks accountability
Proposes GA parallel track if Council paralyzed
Strong vote even if vetoed sends normative signal

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, the Islamic Republic of Iran has been the target of some of the most sophisticated state-sponsored cyber operations in history, including attacks on our nuclear facilities and our petroleum infrastructure. We know from direct experience the damage that cyber weapons can cause. Yet we cannot support this resolution.

Our opposition is not to the norms. Iran supports the protection of civilian infrastructure from cyber operations. Our opposition is to the selective enforcement framework. The state that conducted the most destructive cyber operation in history, targeting Iran's nuclear facilities in an acknowledged act of sabotage, is not only a sponsor of this resolution but would sit on the attribution panel. The state that compromised the personal devices of world leaders is a co-sponsor. This is not governance; it is institutional capture.

Iran insists that any cyber governance framework must be universal in application, free from Security Council veto dynamics, and must equally address offensive cyber capabilities maintained by all states without exception. Until such a framework exists, Iran rejects selective mechanisms that would constrain the cyber capabilities of targeted nations while immunizing those of the powerful. We vote No.
keyPoints: Target of most destructive cyber operation (Stuxnet)
Attackers would sit on attribution panel they sponsor
Demands universal framework free from veto dynamics

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Germany supports this resolution as a critical step in establishing enforceable norms for state behaviour in cyberspace. As a nation whose Bundestag was compromised by a state-sponsored cyber operation in 2015, and whose critical infrastructure faces daily intrusion attempts, Germany has direct national interest in an effective deterrence framework.

Germany wishes to address the argument that attribution is inherently unreliable and therefore cannot serve as a basis for enforcement. This argument does not withstand scrutiny. Multiple states have demonstrated the capacity for technically rigorous attribution with high confidence. The Bundestag hack, the NotPetya attack, the SolarWinds campaign, all were attributed with high confidence through national and collaborative technical analysis. An international panel with access to multiple intelligence streams would achieve even greater reliability.

Germany acknowledges that this resolution may face the veto. If so, we join Brazil in calling for a General Assembly framework. But we urge all Council members to recognize that the protection of hospitals, water systems, and power grids from cyber attack is a humanitarian imperative, not a geopolitical game. Germany votes Yes.
keyPoints: Bundestag compromised in 2015 state cyber operation
Multiple high-confidence attributions prove reliability
Protection of hospitals is humanitarian imperative

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Ghana takes the floor as a nation that is rapidly digitizing its economy and public services. Our digitization makes us vulnerable. Developing nations with less mature cyber defence capabilities are disproportionately affected by state-sponsored cyber operations, yet we have the least capacity to attribute and respond to such attacks.

Ghana supports this resolution precisely because the attribution panel would provide a capability that most nations cannot develop individually. Currently, only a handful of states possess the technical intelligence necessary for confident attribution. This means most victims, particularly in Africa, cannot identify their attackers, much less seek accountability. An international mechanism levels this playing field.

Ghana also supports operative clause 5 on national CERT establishment and international information sharing. Capacity building is essential. We call upon the sponsors to ensure that the resolution's implementation includes dedicated resources for helping developing nations build cyber defence capabilities. Protection of civilian infrastructure from cyber attack should not be a privilege of the technically advanced. Ghana votes Yes.
keyPoints: Developing nations most vulnerable, least able to attribute
International panel levels playing field for victims
Capacity building essential for equitable protection

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, India returns to the floor to announce a change in our position. Having reflected on the arguments presented, and in light of the constructive proposals regarding General Assembly parallel mechanisms and the demonstrated urgency of protecting civilian infrastructure, India will vote Yes on this resolution.

Our decision is influenced by three factors. First, the escalating frequency of state-sponsored cyber operations targeting civilian infrastructure demands action, and perfect should not be the enemy of good. Second, the attribution panel, while imperfect, provides a multilateral mechanism superior to the current situation of unilateral attributions with no institutional accountability. Third, India has itself been a significant victim of cyber operations and has a direct interest in an international deterrence framework.

India's Yes vote is accompanied by expectations. We expect that the attribution panel's mandate, composition, and procedures will be negotiated inclusively, with genuine representation from developing nations. We expect that the panel will address all forms of state-sponsored cyber interference, including espionage against critical infrastructure. And we expect that if the veto prevents Security Council action, the sponsors will pursue the General Assembly track with equal determination. With these expectations recorded, India votes Yes.
keyPoints: Shifted from Abstain due to urgency and pragmatism
Multilateral attribution superior to unilateral status quo
Expects inclusive panel composition with developing nation voice

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, the United States is the largest bilateral donor of water and sanitation assistance globally, having provided over 4 billion dollars in the last decade through USAID programs reaching millions. We share the objective of universal access to clean water and sanitation and recognize the urgency of the water crisis facing many developing nations.

However, the United States has longstanding reservations about the characterization of water access as a binding obligation under international human rights law, as stated in operative clause 1. While we supported General Assembly resolution 64/292 recognizing the right to water, we did so with the understanding that this right does not create obligations for states to provide water services extraterritorially or to subordinate all water management decisions to a single rights-based framework.

The Global Water Access Fund proposed in operative clause 2, capitalized at 50 billion dollars, raises questions about governance, burden-sharing, and conditionality that remain unaddressed. Furthermore, operative clause 4, urging states to prioritize water access over commercial exploitation, could be interpreted to restrict legitimate water resource management including agricultural irrigation and industrial use. The United States will abstain while continuing our substantial bilateral assistance and engaging constructively in implementation discussions.
keyPoints: Reservations on binding obligation characterization
50 billion fund lacks governance and burden-sharing details
Commercial exploitation clause could restrict legitimate use

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, China has achieved one of the most remarkable water access transformations in human history. In thirty years, we have extended piped water to over 500 million rural residents and built water treatment infrastructure serving a population larger than Europe's. China's experience demonstrates that with political will and public investment, the water crisis is solvable.

China supports this resolution and its core premise that access to clean water is a fundamental human right requiring binding state action. The Global Water Access Fund in operative clause 2 represents a welcome investment, though China notes that 50 billion dollars, while substantial, amounts to less than the world spends on bottled water annually. The crisis demands scale.

China also supports the reporting requirements in operative clause 3. Accountability and planning are essential for progress. We offer our own experience in water infrastructure development as a model for South-South cooperation and are prepared to contribute both financially and technically to the fund's implementation. China calls upon all developed nations to meet their responsibilities and votes Yes.
keyPoints: Extended piped water to 500 million rural residents
50 billion less than global bottled water spending
Offers South-South cooperation and technical expertise

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, India's Jal Jeevan Mission represents the largest water infrastructure program in human history, having provided functional household tap water connections to over 140 million rural households since 2019. India understands both the scale of the water challenge and the feasibility of addressing it through determined state action.

India strongly supports this resolution. The right to clean water and sanitation is inseparable from the right to life and the right to an adequate standard of living. For a nation where waterborne diseases still claim hundreds of thousands of lives annually, particularly among children, this is not an abstract legal debate. It is a matter of survival and dignity.

India particularly welcomes the preferential lending provisions in operative clause 5 and the Global Water Access Fund. Water infrastructure requires massive capital investment over decades. Developing nations cannot finance this transformation solely from domestic resources while simultaneously addressing health, education, and energy needs. International solidarity, in the form of concessional finance and technology transfer, is not charity but recognition of shared responsibility for a shared resource under a shared climate threat. India votes Yes.
keyPoints: Jal Jeevan Mission connected 140 million households
Water right inseparable from right to life
International solidarity needed for capital-intensive infrastructure

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, the United Kingdom is a longstanding supporter of the human right to water and sanitation and a major contributor to water access programs through DFID, now FCDO, having helped over 60 million people gain access to clean water over the past decade. Water security is a priority of our international development strategy and a cornerstone of our approach to the Sustainable Development Goals.

The UK supports this resolution. The evidence is unambiguous: 2.2 billion people lack safely managed drinking water, a number that is growing as climate change intensifies hydrological volatility. The SDG 6 targets will not be met without the type of accelerated, binding framework proposed here.

We particularly welcome the Special Rapporteur provision in operative clause 6. Independent monitoring and investigation are essential for accountability. The UK also supports the prioritization of water access over commercial exploitation in operative clause 4, which we interpret as requiring human needs assessments before large-scale commercial water extraction, not as prohibiting all commercial water use. With this interpretive understanding, the UK votes Yes and calls upon all delegations to support universal water access as a matter of basic human dignity.
keyPoints: Helped 60 million gain water access in past decade
SDG 6 targets impossible without binding framework
Interprets commercial clause as requiring needs assessments first

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Ethiopia is the water tower of East Africa, source of the Blue Nile that provides water to hundreds of millions downstream. Yet despite our water wealth, over 30 million Ethiopians still lack access to safe drinking water. Ethiopia understands the paradox that water abundance does not automatically translate into water access. Infrastructure, governance, and investment are required.

Ethiopia strongly supports this resolution on behalf of the African nations facing the most severe water stress. The Global Water Access Fund is critically needed. Africa's water infrastructure deficit is estimated at over 100 billion dollars. Without international support, our nations face an impossible choice between water investment and other development priorities.

Ethiopia also wishes to address the sovereignty concerns related to operative clause 3 on national water plans. We interpret this requirement as supportive of national development planning, not restrictive of sovereign water management decisions. Transboundary water governance, which is central to our region's stability, remains a matter for riparian states to negotiate bilaterally and through regional frameworks. Ethiopia votes Yes and calls upon the international community to match rhetoric with resources.
keyPoints: Water tower of East Africa yet 30 million lack access
Africa's water infrastructure deficit exceeds 100 billion
Transboundary governance remains for riparian states to decide

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, the Marshall Islands speaks as a nation where freshwater is among our most precious and precarious resources. Our low-lying atolls have no rivers and minimal groundwater. We depend on rainfall catchment and fragile freshwater lenses that are increasingly contaminated by saltwater intrusion as sea levels rise. Climate change is directly threatening our right to water.

The Marshall Islands strongly supports this resolution and particularly notes the preamble's recognition that climate crisis exacerbates water scarcity. For small island developing states, the water crisis and the climate crisis are inseparable. Rising seas contaminate our aquifers. Changing rainfall patterns disrupt our catchment systems. More intense droughts leave our communities without drinking water for weeks at a time.

We call upon all delegations to recognize that the right to water requires climate action. A resolution on water access that does not address the climate drivers of water insecurity addresses only symptoms. The Marshall Islands votes Yes and calls upon the Global Water Access Fund to allocate specific resources for climate-resilient water systems in SIDS, including desalination, rainwater harvesting, and aquifer protection. Our right to water depends on our right to a stable climate.
keyPoints: Freshwater lenses contaminated by saltwater intrusion
Water crisis and climate crisis inseparable for SIDS
Calls for climate-resilient water systems in fund allocation

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, the Russian Federation possesses the largest freshwater reserves of any nation on Earth, including Lake Baikal, which alone contains twenty percent of the world's unfrozen surface freshwater. Russia approaches this resolution not as a nation facing water scarcity but as one concerned about the implications of binding international obligations regarding natural resource management.

Russia has reservations about operative clause 1, which declares water access a binding obligation under international human rights law. While we do not dispute the importance of water access, the creation of new binding obligations through General Assembly resolutions without treaty negotiation raises procedural concerns. The General Assembly recommends; it does not legislate.

However, Russia acknowledges the humanitarian importance of this resolution and does not wish to be seen as opposing the objective of universal water access. We therefore abstain rather than vote No, while placing on record our understanding that this resolution does not create extraterritorial obligations for water-abundant states to transfer resources to water-scarce states without bilateral agreement. Russia remains committed to humanitarian water assistance on a voluntary bilateral basis.
keyPoints: Holds 20% of world unfrozen surface freshwater
GA cannot create binding obligations without treaty
No extraterritorial obligation to transfer water resources

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Brazil possesses approximately twelve percent of the world's freshwater in the Amazon basin and has achieved near-universal urban water access. Yet we recognize that water remains a challenge in our semi-arid Northeast, where transposition projects and desalination investments continue. Brazil's experience demonstrates that even water-rich nations must invest continuously in equitable access.

Brazil strongly supports this resolution. We view the right to water as the most fundamental of economic, social, and cultural rights, without which no other right can be meaningfully exercised. The right to education means little for a child debilitated by waterborne disease. The right to health is meaningless without clean water for hygiene and sanitation.

Brazil also wishes to address the sovereignty dimension. We interpret operative clause 4 on prioritizing access over commercial exploitation as referring to domestic water allocation decisions, not as restricting sovereign rights over transboundary resources. Brazil maintains its sovereign right to manage the Amazon basin's water resources while supporting the principle that within every nation, human needs take precedence over commercial profit. With this understanding, Brazil votes Yes and pledges technical cooperation with nations seeking to expand water access.
keyPoints: 12% of world freshwater, near-universal urban access
Water is most fundamental right enabling all others
Domestic allocation prioritizes humans over commerce

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Saudi Arabia is among the most water-scarce nations on Earth. We receive less than 100 millimeters of annual rainfall and have no permanent rivers or freshwater lakes. Despite these extreme conditions, Saudi Arabia has invested massively in desalination technology, becoming the world's largest producer of desalinated water with over 30 plants providing 70 percent of our drinking water.

Saudi Arabia supports this resolution as a nation that has transformed water scarcity into water security through sustained investment and technological innovation. We demonstrate that water access is ultimately a matter of political will and resource allocation, not simply geography. However, we note that our solution required enormous capital, over 25 billion dollars in desalination infrastructure alone, capital that most water-scarce developing nations do not possess.

This is precisely why the Global Water Access Fund is essential. Saudi Arabia is prepared to contribute to the fund and to share our desalination expertise with other water-scarce nations. We also support research into next-generation desalination technologies that reduce energy costs and environmental impact. The Kingdom votes Yes and calls upon all water-abundant nations that have benefited from nature's geography to demonstrate solidarity with those that have not.
keyPoints: World largest desalination producer despite extreme scarcity
Invested 25 billion in desalination infrastructure
Prepared to contribute to fund and share expertise

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Germany approaches the right to water from both a development cooperation perspective and as a nation that has experienced the consequences of water commodification. Our experience with water privatization in the 1990s and early 2000s, and the subsequent remunicipalizations in Berlin and other cities, demonstrated that water services managed primarily for profit risk excluding the most vulnerable.

Germany strongly supports operative clause 4 on prioritizing water access over commercial exploitation. This reflects a principle that Germany has enshrined in our own policies: water is a public good, not a commodity. While private sector involvement in water infrastructure can be beneficial, the governance framework must ensure that access for all takes precedence over returns for shareholders.

Germany pledges to increase our water sector development cooperation, currently approximately 800 million euros annually, and to support the Global Water Access Fund both financially and technically. We also strongly support the Special Rapporteur provision as an accountability mechanism. Germany votes Yes and urges all EU member states to support a resolution that embodies the values of solidarity and universal human dignity upon which our Union is founded.
keyPoints: Remunicipalizations proved water is public good not commodity
Pledges to increase 800 million euro water cooperation
Private sector must not prioritize returns over access

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, Somalia speaks as a nation in acute water crisis. Recurring droughts, exacerbated by climate change, have displaced millions and created conditions where families walk hours for water that is neither safe nor sufficient. In 2022, Somalia experienced its worst drought in four decades, with 80 percent of the country affected and millions facing acute water insecurity.

For Somalia, this resolution is not about legal frameworks or institutional design. It is about the survival of our children. Every day that we debate, children in the Horn of Africa die from waterborne diseases that are entirely preventable with basic infrastructure. The 50 billion dollar Global Water Access Fund, if properly allocated, could transform the water landscape of the entire Horn of Africa within a decade.

Somalia calls upon this Assembly to move beyond aspiration to action. We have enough resolutions, declarations, and targets. What we lack is investment, infrastructure, and political will. Somalia votes Yes and implores the international community to ensure that this resolution leads to concrete resource flows, not merely another document gathering dust in the UN archives. Our people cannot drink resolutions. They need water.
keyPoints: 2022 worst drought in 40 years affecting 80% of country
Children dying daily from preventable waterborne diseases
People cannot drink resolutions - need concrete investment

speeches.speech:
Mr. President, the United States returns to the floor to announce a change in our position. Having listened to the powerful testimony of delegations from water-scarce nations, and having consulted with partners on the interpretive understandings offered during this debate, the United States will vote Yes on this resolution.

Our shift is based on the following understanding. First, operative clause 1 on binding obligations is interpreted as reaffirming existing obligations under the ICESCR and customary international law regarding progressive realization, not as creating new immediate extraterritorial duties. Second, operative clause 4 on commercial exploitation is understood as establishing a prioritization framework within domestic water allocation, not as restricting all commercial water use.

The United States has invested billions in global water access and will continue to do so. The water crisis is too urgent and too devastating, particularly for the most vulnerable populations, to permit abstention on a resolution that enjoys near-universal support. The United States votes Yes and commits to constructive engagement in the fund's governance design, ensuring accountability, transparency, and results-based allocation. Clean water is too fundamental a human need to be held hostage to legal technicalities.
keyPoints: Shifted from Abstain based on interpretive understandings
Interprets obligations as progressive realization framework
Water too fundamental to be hostage to legal technicalities
